_meta.note: R+L=J cluster: mint r-plus-l-equals-j (concept.theory) + enrich existing knight-of-the-laughing-tree-theories stub. Sources: ASX videos kHqzFwodZqQ (R+L=J) + UrhqmMRv1gQ (Rhaegar, R+L=J-cluster beats only, Azor-Ahai beats held) for the first theory; eUVr_lUxYf8 (Knight of the Laughing Tree) for the second. All quotes byte-verified against sources/chapters/*.md at cited lines.

edges.quote: He had lived his lies for fourteen years, yet they still haunted him at night.
edges.note: Ned's fourteen-year-old lie (Jon is fourteen at AGOT's start) — the theory reads this as the concealed truth of Jon's parentage.

edges.quote: He dreamt an old dream, of three knights in white cloaks, and a tower long fallen, and Lyanna in her bed of blood.
edges.note: Ned's Tower of Joy dream; the theory reads Lyanna's 'bed of blood' as childbirth, not a death-from-violence bed.

edges.quote: A storm of rose petals blew across a blood-streaked sky, as blue as the eyes of death.
edges.note: The ToJ dream fuses blue-rose (Lyanna) imagery with the fight over her; ties the event to the recurring blue-flower/Lyanna motif the theory leans on.

edges.quote: Prince Rhaegar Targaryen urged his horse past his own wife, the Dornish princess Elia Martell, to lay the queen of beauty’s laurel in Lyanna’s lap.
edges.note: Rhaegar publicly choosing Lyanna over his wife Elia at Harrenhal — the theory's starting point for the Rhaegar/Lyanna relationship.

edges.quote: Rhaegar had chosen Lyanna Stark of Winterfell. Barristan Selmy would have made a different choice.
edges.note: Cross-book (Barristan POV, ADWD) corroboration of the Harrenhal choice, independent of Ned's account.

edges.quote: A blue flower grew from a chink in a wall of ice, and filled the air with sweetness.
edges.note: House of the Undying vision; the theory reads the blue flower (Lyanna's flower) at 'a wall of ice' as pointing to something of Lyanna's at the Wall — read as Jon. Tier-4 (S214 adjudication, fresh-verify concurring): stacked prophetic-vision symbolism — a canonical fandom hint but interpretive at every step, weaker than the tier-3 edges.

edges.quote: I shall take no wife, hold no lands, father no children. I shall wear no crowns and win no glory.
edges.note: Complicates the theory's throne-claim extension (not the base parentage claim): Jon's Night's Watch vow forecloses any crown even if he is Rhaegar's trueborn son.

edges.quote: He could see it still: a crown of winter roses, blue as frost.
edges.note: The crowning itself plants the blue-winter-rose motif on Lyanna — the symbol the theory tracks through Ned's dreams, her crypt statue, and Dany's wall-of-ice vision. (S214 adjudication: requoted from the crypt-statue line to the crowning's own line so the quote matches the source event node; the statue quote stays in the theory node body.)

edges.quote: ‘That’s my father’s man you’re kicking,’ howled the she-wolf.
edges.note: Lyanna ('the she-wolf') is the one who rescues Howland Reed from the squires, establishing her motive to avenge his honor — the theory's basis for identifying her as the mystery knight.

edges.quote: the Knight of the Laughing Tree spoke in a booming voice through his helm, saying, ‘Teach your squires honor, that shall be ransom enough.’
edges.note: The core incident: an anonymous knight avenges Howland Reed's mistreatment at Harrenhal by defeating his attackers' knights and demanding they teach their squires honor.

edges.quote: The king was wroth, and even sent his son the dragon prince to seek the man, but all they ever found was his painted shield, hanging abandoned in a tree.
edges.note: Aerys sends Rhaegar himself to unmask the mystery knight; the theory reads this as the likely start of Rhaegar and Lyanna's secret relationship, since Rhaegar would have been well-placed to identify her.

edges.quote: “She was,” said Meera, “but that’s a sadder story.”
edges.note: Meera confirms — in response to Bran's guess that the mystery knight should crown 'the wolf maid' queen of love and beauty — that the wolf maid (Lyanna) WAS later crowned queen of love and beauty, textually tying the Knight of the Laughing Tree episode to Rhaegar's later, better-known crowning of Lyanna at the same tourney.

edges.quote: “Are you certain you never heard this tale before, Bran?” asked Jojen. “Your lord father never told it to you?”
edges.note: Jojen's surprise that Ned never told Bran this story is the textual hook for the sub-theories that Ned himself was close to (or was) the mystery knight; weak/suggestive, not conclusive.
